title: Integration of body systems
summary: The body is not a bag of independent organs — it is a single coordinated system whose parts constantly exchange information. This HL lesson builds the whole of C3.1: the body as an integrated hierarchy of subsystems, the nervous and endocrine systems as the two contrasting control-and-communication pathways, the brain as the central integrating centre, negative feedback as the logic that keeps physiology stable, and emergent properties that appear only when subsystems act together. It works through the classic integration examples — heart rate, blood glucose and the adrenaline-driven fight-or-flight response — and every explanation is laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards Paper 2 marks: one distinct valid point, one mark.
learningObjectives: Describe the body as an integrated system of interacting organs organised into a hierarchy of subsystems.
Compare and contrast the nervous and endocrine systems as the two control and communication systems, and explain how they are integrated.
Explain the role of the brain as the central integrating centre that coordinates responses.
Explain how negative feedback coordinates physiological processes and returns variables to a set point.
Analyse worked integration examples — control of heart rate, of blood glucose, and the fight-or-flight response — showing multiple systems acting together.
Present explanations as distinct, creditable points so that each valid idea earns a mark.

simpleExplanation.title: One Body, Many Talking Parts
simpleExplanation.summary: Your organs do not work in isolation. They are wired and chemically linked into a single coordinated system, run by two communication networks — fast electrical nerves and slower chemical hormones — with the brain acting as the control room that reads the situation and decides the response.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a large airport. The nervous system is the radio between the control tower and each pilot: instant, precise, aimed at one aircraft at a time, and over the moment the message is passed. The endocrine system is the airport-wide public-address announcement: slower to compose, broadcast to everyone at once, and its effect lingers long after it is spoken. The brain is the control tower itself — it gathers reports from every corner, compares them against the plan, and sends out the two kinds of message together so the whole airport behaves as one operation rather than a crowd of separate gates.
steps: The body is organised as a hierarchy: molecules build organelles, cells, tissues, organs and organ systems, and the whole organism behaves in ways no single part could — an emergent property.

Two systems carry the control signals: the nervous system uses fast, short-lived electrical impulses to precise targets; the endocrine system uses slower, longer-lasting hormones carried in the blood to many targets at once.
The brain is the central integrating centre: it receives sensory information, compares it with set points, and coordinates both nervous and endocrine outputs.

Negative feedback ties it together — when a variable (heart rate, blood glucose) drifts from its set point, the response opposes the change and brings it back, keeping the internal environment stable.

flashcards.back: The coordinated interaction of many organs and organ systems so that the organism functions as a single, regulated whole rather than a set of independent parts.
A control-and-communication system using electrical impulses carried along neurons. It is FAST, SHORT-LIVED, and PRECISELY TARGETED to specific cells across synapses.

A control-and-communication system using HORMONES — chemical messengers secreted by glands into the blood. It is SLOWER to act, LONGER-LASTING, and WIDESPREAD, reaching all cells with the right receptor.

The brain (especially regions such as the hypothalamus and medulla oblongata) receives sensory input, compares it with set points, and coordinates both nervous and endocrine outputs — integrating the whole body's response.
A control mechanism in which a change in a variable triggers a response that OPPOSES the change, returning the variable towards its set point. It is the basis of homeostatic stability.
The target value of a regulated variable (e.g. core body temperature ~37°C, blood glucose ~5 mmol dm⁻³) around which negative feedback holds the variable within narrow limits.
The body is organised in nested levels — molecule → organelle → cell → tissue → organ → organ system → organism. Each level is a subsystem of the level above and is itself built from the level below.

Properties that arise from the INTERACTIONS between components of a system and are not shown by any component alone. Consciousness, homeostasis and coordinated movement emerge from integrated subsystems.

Part of the brainstem containing the cardiovascular and respiratory control centres. It receives sensory information and adjusts heart rate and breathing via the autonomic nerves — a key integrating centre.

Two branches of the autonomic nervous system. SYMPATHETIC nerves speed up heart rate (and support fight-or-flight); PARASYMPATHETIC (vagus) nerves slow it down. They act antagonistically to fine-tune output.

A hormone released by the adrenal glands (medulla) in response to stress or exercise. It prepares the body for action — raising heart rate, dilating airways and releasing glucose — the endocrine arm of fight-or-flight.

Antagonistic hormones from the pancreas. INSULIN lowers blood glucose (promoting uptake and glycogen storage); GLUCAGON raises it (promoting glycogen breakdown). Together they hold glucose near its set point by negative feedback.

flashcards.front: Exam Trap: fast/slow the wrong way round
flashcards.back: Nervous = fast, brief, localised. Endocrine = slow, prolonged, widespread. Swapping these is one of the most penalised errors when comparing the two systems.
Homeostatic control is NEGATIVE feedback — the response opposes the change. Saying the response 'increases the change further' describes positive feedback and loses the mark.

quickCheck.prompt: Which set of features correctly describes the nervous system compared with the endocrine system?
options: Slow, long-lasting and widespread
Fast, short-lived and localised to specific target cells
Fast, long-lasting and reaching all cells in the blood
Slow, short-lived and targeted to one organ
quickCheck.answer: Fast, short-lived and localised to specific target cells

quickCheck.prompt: A hormone travels in the blood, acts over minutes to hours, and affects many organs at once. This best describes the:
options: Nervous system, because impulses spread through the body
Endocrine system, because hormones are slow, long-lasting and widespread
Nervous system, because it reaches all cells
Endocrine system, because it targets only one synapse
quickCheck.answer: Endocrine system, because hormones are slow, long-lasting and widespread

quickCheck.prompt: In negative feedback control of blood glucose, a RISE in blood glucose triggers a response that:
options: Raises blood glucose further, amplifying the change
Lowers blood glucose back towards the set point
Has no effect until glucose is dangerously high
Switches off all hormone release permanently
quickCheck.answer: Lowers blood glucose back towards the set point

quickCheck.prompt: Which structure acts as the central integrating centre that coordinates both nervous and endocrine responses?
options: The adrenal gland
The spinal cord alone
The brain (e.g. hypothalamus and medulla oblongata)
The pancreas
quickCheck.answer: The brain (e.g. hypothalamus and medulla oblongata)

quickCheck.prompt: Homeostasis and coordinated movement are best described as emergent properties because they:
options: Are produced by a single specialised organ acting alone
Arise from interactions between many subsystems, not from any part on its own
Only appear in individual cells in isolation
Are the same thing as negative feedback
quickCheck.answer: Arise from interactions between many subsystems, not from any part on its own

faq.q: What is the single clearest difference between the nervous and endocrine systems?
faq.a: Speed and reach. The nervous system sends electrical impulses along neurons to precise target cells — the effect arrives in milliseconds and stops almost as soon as the impulses stop, so it is fast, brief and localised. The endocrine system secretes hormones into the blood, which carries them everywhere, so the response takes seconds to minutes to build, lasts much longer, and can affect many organs at once. A useful shorthand: nerves are like a phone call to one person; hormones are like a message broadcast to everyone, and it keeps echoing for a while.

faq.q: If the two systems are so different, how do they work as one?
faq.a: They are integrated through shared control centres in the brain and through overlapping targets. In the heart-rate example the medulla oblongata sends nervous signals for instant adjustment AND triggers the adrenal glands to release adrenaline for a sustained effect, so the same goal is met on two timescales. The hypothalamus is the master link — it is nervous tissue that also controls the pituitary gland, literally converting nervous information into hormonal instructions. That is why we treat the body as one coordinated system, not two separate ones.

faq.q: Why is negative feedback so central to integration?
faq.a: Because it is the logic that keeps a variable stable while many systems act on it. A receptor detects a change from the set point, the brain (integrating centre) compares and coordinates a response, and effectors produce an output that OPPOSES the original change — pulling the variable back. Once the variable returns to the set point, the stimulus fades and the response is switched off. Every integration example in this topic — heart rate, blood glucose, temperature — is a negative-feedback loop, which is why examiners expect the stimulus → receptor → centre → effector → response → return-to-set-point language.

faq.q: What exactly is an 'emergent property' in this topic?
faq.a: It is a property of the whole system that none of its parts shows on its own — it emerges from how the parts interact. A single neuron cannot be conscious; a single muscle fibre cannot walk; a single gland cannot maintain homeostasis. But when these subsystems are integrated in the correct hierarchy, coordinated behaviours like homeostasis, movement and cognition appear. The point examiners want is that emergent properties come from INTERACTION between subsystems, so you cannot predict them by studying any one part in isolation.

sections.content: It is tempting to learn the body one organ at a time — the heart here, the pancreas there, the brain somewhere else. But biology at HL asks a harder question: how do all these parts behave as a single, self-regulating whole? Lift a heavy bag and within one second your heart is already beating faster, your breathing has deepened, and glucose is flowing to your muscles — three organ systems responding together, before you have consciously decided anything. C3.1 is about that coordination. We will see the body as a hierarchy of subsystems whose integration produces emergent properties; meet the two control-and-communication systems, nervous and endocrine, and pin down exactly how they differ and how they combine; place the brain at the centre as the integrating hub; and use negative feedback to explain three classic examples — heart rate, blood glucose and the fight-or-flight response. Throughout, the explanations are written the way our marking engine actually awards Paper 2 marks: one distinct, valid point earns one mark.
The body as an integrated system

A living body is more than the sum of its organs. Its parts are connected — physically, electrically and chemically — so that a change in one is sensed and answered by others. This is what we mean by integration: organs and organ systems interacting so that the organism functions as one regulated unit. The circulatory system, for example, only delivers oxygen usefully because the respiratory system loads that oxygen, the nervous system sets the pace of both, and the endocrine system adjusts them over the longer term. No single system maintains life; life is maintained by their coordinated action.

items: The body is a coordinated system of interacting organs, not a collection of independent parts.
Integration means the systems constantly exchange information and adjust one another.
Coordination is achieved by two control-and-communication systems: **nervous** and **endocrine**.
The **brain** acts as the central integrating centre that links sensory input to coordinated output.

sections.content: Hierarchy of subsystems and emergent properties

The body is organised as a nested hierarchy: molecules assemble into organelles, organelles into cells, cells into tissues, tissues into organs, organs into organ systems, and organ systems into the whole organism. Each level is a subsystem of the level above it and is itself built from the level below. The crucial idea is that new properties appear at each level that the components below do not possess — these are emergent properties. A single neuron cannot think; a single muscle fibre cannot run; a single pancreatic cell cannot regulate blood glucose. Yet when these subsystems are correctly integrated, thought, movement and homeostasis emerge. Emergent properties arise from the INTERACTIONS between components, which is precisely why you cannot predict how the whole body behaves by studying one organ in isolation — integration itself generates the behaviour.

items: **Hierarchy:** molecule → organelle → cell → tissue → organ → organ system → organism.
Each level is a subsystem — assembled from the level below and part of the level above.
**Emergent properties** are properties of the whole that no single part shows, e.g. homeostasis, coordinated movement, consciousness.
Emergent properties come from INTERACTIONS between subsystems, not from any component alone.

sections.content: The two control systems: nervous vs endocrine

The body coordinates itself using two communication systems that are deliberately different, because different jobs need different kinds of message. The nervous system transmits electrical impulses along neurons to specific target cells, releasing neurotransmitters across synapses. Its signals travel in milliseconds, act on precise targets, and stop almost as soon as the stimulation stops — it is fast, localised and short-lived, ideal for rapid, pinpoint responses like adjusting a heartbeat or pulling a hand from a flame. The endocrine system works chemically: glands secrete hormones into the blood, which distributes them throughout the body. Only cells with the matching receptor respond, but those cells may be spread across many organs. Hormonal responses take seconds to minutes to build, last far longer, and are widespread — ideal for sustained, whole-body adjustments like growth, metabolism or a prolonged stress response. Neither system is 'better'; the body needs both, and it integrates them.

items: **Nervous system:** electrical impulses along neurons → FAST, SHORT-LIVED, PRECISELY LOCALISED to target cells.
**Endocrine system:** hormones carried in the blood → SLOWER to act, LONGER-LASTING, WIDESPREAD.
Nervous signalling suits rapid, pinpoint responses; endocrine signalling suits sustained, whole-body responses.
The two are complementary — the body uses both, on different timescales, for the same goals.
**Integration point:** the hypothalamus is nervous tissue that also controls the pituitary gland, converting nervous information directly into hormonal instructions.

sections.question: Compare and contrast the nervous system and the endocrine system as control and communication systems. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
Both the nervous and endocrine systems are control and communication systems that carry information between different parts of the body to coordinate its responses. However, the nervous system transmits information as electrical impulses along neurons, whereas the endocrine system transmits information as hormones carried in the blood. Nervous signals travel very rapidly and act on precise target cells across synapses, while hormonal signals travel more slowly and reach widespread targets throughout the body. The effect of a nervous impulse is short-lived, ending soon after the stimulus stops, whereas the effect of a hormone is longer-lasting. The two systems are integrated — for example, both are coordinated by the brain and can act on the same organ, such as the heart — so the body responds as one system.

---
**How our marking engine awards the marks** — each distinct valid point scores 1 (maximum 4):

- **Point 1:** both are control/communication systems that coordinate the body (a valid similarity).
- **Point 2:** nervous uses electrical impulses along neurons / endocrine uses hormones in the blood.
- **Point 3:** nervous is fast / endocrine is slower (to act).
- **Point 4:** nervous is localised/precise target cells / endocrine is widespread.
- **Point 5:** nervous effect is short-lived / endocrine effect is longer-lasting.
- **Point 6:** the systems are integrated / coordinated by the brain / act on the same organ.

Six creditable points are shown for a 4-mark question, so the engine caps the award at 4 — there is room to miss points and still score full marks. A 'compare and contrast' question needs BOTH sides of a contrast in the same point (e.g. 'nervous fast, endocrine slow'); stating only one half earns the point once but a bare 'nervous is fast' with no comparison is weaker. Repeating the same contrast in two forms scores once.

sections.content: The brain as the central integrating centre

For two systems to act as one, something must gather the incoming information, compare it against what the body needs, and decide the coordinated output. That is the role of the brain as the central integrating centre. Sensory receptors throughout the body send information to the brain; specific regions process it and issue commands through both nervous and endocrine channels. The medulla oblongata in the brainstem, for instance, receives information about blood pressure and blood chemistry and adjusts heart rate and breathing through the autonomic nerves. The hypothalamus monitors variables such as temperature, water balance and blood glucose, and — being nervous tissue that also controls the pituitary gland — it can command hormonal responses as well as nervous ones. Because a single centre coordinates both output pathways, the fast nervous adjustment and the slower endocrine adjustment pull in the same direction rather than working against each other. This is the essence of integration: one centre, comparing against set points, orchestrating many effectors.

items: The brain receives sensory information from receptors across the body.
It compares this information against internal **set points**.
It coordinates responses through BOTH the nervous and endocrine systems.
The **medulla oblongata** integrates heart rate and breathing; the **hypothalamus** links nervous input to hormonal output via the pituitary.
Because one centre coordinates both pathways, fast and slow responses reinforce each other.

sections.content: Negative feedback coordinates physiological processes

The logic that keeps an integrated variable stable is negative feedback. When a regulated variable drifts away from its set point, a receptor detects the change and informs the integrating centre (the brain), which activates effectors that produce a response OPPOSING the original change — driving the variable back towards the set point. As the variable returns, the stimulus weakens and the response is switched off. The word 'negative' captures the key idea: the response counteracts the change rather than amplifying it. This is why the internal environment stays within narrow limits despite constant disturbance, and it is the shared pattern behind every integration example in this topic. Learn the loop as a sentence you can reproduce in any answer: stimulus → receptor detects → brain (integrating centre) coordinates → effector responds → response opposes the change → variable returns to set point.
\text{stimulus} \rightarrow \text{receptor} \rightarrow \text{integrating centre (brain)} \rightarrow \text{effector} \rightarrow \text{response opposes change} \rightarrow \text{return to set point}
Integration example 1 — control of blood glucose

Blood glucose is held near a set point of about 5 mmol dm⁻³ by two antagonistic hormones from the pancreas, coordinated by negative feedback. After a meal, blood glucose rises; this is detected by cells in the pancreas, which secrete INSULIN. Insulin causes body cells (especially liver and muscle) to take up glucose and the liver to convert glucose to glycogen for storage, so blood glucose falls back towards the set point. Between meals or during exercise, blood glucose falls; the pancreas now secretes GLUCAGON, which stimulates the liver to break glycogen back down into glucose and release it, raising blood glucose towards the set point. Each hormone's response opposes the change that triggered it — a textbook negative-feedback loop — and because the two hormones act antagonistically, the system can correct deviations in either direction. This is endocrine coordination: slow-to-start but sustained, widespread through the blood, and self-limiting once the set point is restored.

items: **Set point:** blood glucose ≈ 5 mmol dm⁻³, monitored by the pancreas.
**Glucose rises → insulin:** cells take up glucose; liver stores it as glycogen → glucose falls.
**Glucose falls → glucagon:** liver breaks glycogen down to glucose and releases it → glucose rises.
Insulin and glucagon are **antagonistic**, correcting deviations in both directions.
Each response OPPOSES the change — negative feedback keeps glucose near the set point.

sections.content: Integration example 2 — the fight-or-flight response (adrenaline)

A sudden threat shows the nervous and endocrine systems working together most vividly. The brain perceives danger and, through the sympathetic nervous system, triggers an immediate response — but it also signals the adrenal glands to release the hormone ADRENALINE (epinephrine) into the blood. Adrenaline reaches many organs at once and prepares the body for vigorous action: heart rate and breathing rate rise, airways dilate, blood is diverted to skeletal muscles, and the liver releases glucose to fuel them. Here the two systems are complementary in timing. The nervous (sympathetic) route acts within a fraction of a second for the instant reaction; the endocrine (adrenaline) route builds over seconds but keeps the body primed for far longer, long after the initial nervous burst has faded. The same threat is met on two timescales by two systems coordinated from one centre — a clear demonstration of integration producing a whole-body response no single system could deliver alone.

items: A perceived threat is processed by the **brain**, which activates the **sympathetic nervous system** for an instant response.
The brain also triggers the **adrenal glands** to release **adrenaline (epinephrine)** into the blood.
Adrenaline is **widespread and longer-lasting**: it raises heart and breathing rate, diverts blood to muscles, and releases glucose from the liver.
**Nervous = instant but brief; endocrine = slower but sustained** — the two timescales together prepare the body for action.
One integrating centre coordinates both systems, so their effects reinforce rather than conflict.

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Reversing the fast/slow contrast** — the nervous system is fast, brief and localised; the endocrine system is slow, prolonged and widespread. Swapping these features is the most common comparison error and loses marks.

**Calling homeostatic control 'positive feedback'** — homeostasis works by NEGATIVE feedback: the response OPPOSES the change. Saying the response amplifies the change describes positive feedback and scores nothing.

**Naming the response but not the loop** — for negative-feedback marks you must show stimulus → receptor → integrating centre → effector → response → return to set point, not just 'insulin lowers glucose'.
**Confusing insulin and glucagon** — insulin LOWERS blood glucose (uptake and storage); glucagon RAISES it (glycogen breakdown). Reversing them inverts the whole loop.

**Treating the systems as separate** — the question is usually about INTEGRATION. Describe how nervous and endocrine outputs are coordinated (e.g. from the medulla or hypothalamus), not two unrelated processes.
**Saying hormones target 'everywhere' indiscriminately** — hormones travel everywhere in the blood but only cells with the matching RECEPTOR respond. State the receptor idea to be precise.
**Describing emergent properties as belonging to one organ** — emergent properties arise from INTERACTIONS between subsystems; they cannot be attributed to a single part acting alone.
**Vague 'the brain controls it' with no mechanism** — name the centre and the pathway (e.g. medulla → autonomic nerves; hypothalamus → pituitary → hormone) to earn the integration mark.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

C3.1 explanations are marked analytically: each distinct valid biological point is worth one mark, and equivalent correct wording is accepted. There is no all-or-nothing penalty — every creditable line stands on its own, so partial understanding still earns partial credit. Study how each mark below is tied to a specific, named idea (a system, a signal, a direction of effect) rather than to loose phrasing.

sections.question: Explain how the nervous and endocrine systems work together to control heart rate. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
The medulla oblongata in the brainstem receives sensory information about the body's state — such as blood pressure and blood carbon dioxide/pH — and acts as the integrating centre for heart rate. It sends nervous signals through the autonomic nervous system: sympathetic nerves speed the heart up and parasympathetic (vagus) nerves slow it down, adjusting the rate rapidly and precisely. At the same time, in situations such as exercise or stress, the adrenal glands release the hormone adrenaline (epinephrine) into the blood. Adrenaline travels to the heart and increases heart rate, and because it is carried in the blood its effect is more widespread and longer-lasting than the nervous signals. In this way the fast nervous adjustment and the slower, sustained hormonal effect are coordinated by the brain so that heart rate is controlled to meet the body's changing demands.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** — each distinct valid point scores 1 (maximum 4); accept equivalents:

- **Point 1:** the medulla (oblongata) / brain detects or receives the signal and acts as the integrating centre.
- **Point 2:** nerves (sympathetic/parasympathetic, autonomic) adjust heart rate rapidly / the nervous route is fast and precise.
- **Point 3:** the adrenal glands release adrenaline/epinephrine into the blood.
- **Point 4:** adrenaline increases heart rate, giving a longer-lasting / more widespread effect than the nervous signal.
- **Further creditable points (still capped at 4):** sympathetic speeds up AND parasympathetic slows down (antagonistic control); the two systems are integrated/coordinated by the same centre so their effects reinforce one another.

**A for the correct step.** The award is 'A for the correct step' — a point is credited whenever the correct system, signal or direction of effect is named, and correct equivalents are accepted (e.g. 'noradrenaline/sympathetic transmitter' for the nervous speed-up, 'brainstem' for medulla). More than four creditable points are available, so a student can miss one and still reach full marks.

**What the engine rejects.** 'The brain makes the heart beat faster' with no system named scores nothing for integration — it identifies neither the nervous nor the endocrine pathway. Reversing the contrast ('nerves are slow, hormones are fast') is not credited. Repeating one idea in two forms ('adrenaline speeds the heart' and 'the hormone increases heart rate') scores once, because the marks are for DISTINCT ideas.

**Bottom line:** to secure all 4 marks, name the integrating centre (medulla/brain), the fast nervous adjustment (sympathetic/parasympathetic nerves), the hormonal route (adrenal glands release adrenaline into the blood), and the contrast in effect (widespread/longer-lasting). Each distinct valid point is one mark.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — point by point, one mark per distinct valid idea, capped at the marks available, with equivalents accepted. So write in separate, creditable statements: name the integrating centre, name the nervous pathway and that it is fast, name the adrenal/adrenaline route, and state that the hormonal effect is widespread and longer-lasting. Do not bury two systems inside one vague sentence about 'the body' — the mark scheme rewards each named system, signal and direction of effect, and showing them separately is what protects your score.
Where this leads

Integration is the organising idea behind the rest of human physiology at HL. The same nervous-plus-endocrine logic you have used for heart rate governs blood pressure, breathing, water balance (osmoregulation via ADH from the hypothalamus and pituitary) and thermoregulation. Every one of these is a negative-feedback loop with the brain as the integrating centre and two output systems working on different timescales. Master the pattern here — hierarchy and emergent properties, fast nervous versus slow endocrine signalling, one integrating centre, feedback that opposes change — and you have a template that explains coordinated control anywhere in the body.
